macro_segments.title: Introduction: Three Categories of Blemishes and Conceptual Framework

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on three categories of blemishes: actual מום, אינו שוה בזרעו של אהרן, and Rabbinic מראית עין
Rambam's conceptualization: calling אינו שוה בזרעו של אהרן a type of מום and the distinction from the Gemara's reconceptualization
Question of aesthetics versus objective blemish: analysis of במה (altar), ברכת כהנים (priestly blessing), and moral issues
Distinguishing ברכת כהנים from עבודה: distraction-based criteria versus formal blemish requirements
Rambam's enumeration of 140 blemishes for humans and the breadth of the category

macro_segments.title: Mishna 43b: גיבן (eyebrow/back blemish) and the Gemara's Analysis of Terminology

micro_segments.title: Mishna: אין לו גבינים or only one eyebrow; Rabbi Dosa's interpretation of long hanging eyebrows; Rabbi Chanina ben Antignos on two spines and two backbones
Gemara: הגיבן דאינו משמע—resolving the apparent contradiction that גיבן means both 'no eyebrows' and 'many eyebrows'
Rava's explanation that the word גיבן fundamentally means 'many' and the או (or) teaches the opposite extreme
Tanai and Rav on the viability of a fetus with two backs: not considered viable, hence not a living creature in women or a viable animal
Rav Shimi bar Chiya's question and Rav's answer: the two backs actually means a curved/bent spine, not literally two separate spines

macro_segments.title: Nose and Eye Blemishes: חרום and בעינו

micro_segments.title: Mishna: חרום—lacking a nose bridge between the eyes; eye placement variations (high, low, one high one low); cross-eyed vision; sun sensitivity (סכיף שמש); zagdan and tziran conditions; fallen eyelashes as מראית עין
Gemara discussion of חרום: depressed nose, sealed nose (fused nostrils), dripping nose; the word או extends to any nose problem
Rabbi Yossi limiting חרום to the case where the nose is so depressed that both eyes can be painted at once; others rejecting this limitation
Eye placement: clarifying that שעיניו למעלה/למטה refers to physical location in the skull, not direction of gaze

Tanai conceptual categories: iver (blind or severely limited vision), dak (film/cataract causing blurred vision), tavlul (mixing of white into pupil), and be'eino (encompassing eye location and tracking issues)
Rava's analysis: why all these categories are necessary; different types of vision impairment require different classifications
Zagdayan (mismatched eyes): one black one white or big eye versus small eye; the Tosafot discussion on Greek transliteration of זוג (pair)
Tziran conditions: overly circular eyes, teary eyes at various degrees (dumpet, lofot, tzerudot), constantly moving eyes, excessively long eyelashes, or missing eyelashes
Fallen eyelashes: distinction between missing only hair versus missing roots; if roots remain it is only מראית עין, if roots are gone it is a true מום
Mishna: giant eyes as large as a calf's or tiny as a duck's; body disproportionate to limbs or vice versa; nose disproportionate to other limbs
Tzomem (sponge-like ears/cauliflower ear) and tzome'a (small ears) as blemishes
Lip protrusions: upper lip exceeding lower or vice versa as a מום
Gemara: Moses's height (ten amot) and the question of disproportionality; clarifying that amot are measured by standard measure, not Moses's own arm length

macro_segments.title: Animal Blemishes vs Human Blemishes: Horns, Hooves, and Ears

micro_segments.title: Animals without typical blemishes: goat without horns, sheep with horns, large ears, are not considered מומים for animals despite being problems for humans
Gemara on horn and hoof removal: distinction between removal with marrow bone (more severe) versus cutting above the marrow (less severe); redemption status varies
Para Aduma (red heifer): black tips on horns or hooves can be fixed by cutting without affecting the animal's status, illustrating different severity levels

macro_segments.title: Abdominal and Systemic Conditions: כרסו צבה, Epilepsy, and Genital Abnormalities

micro_segments.title: Mishna: breasts hanging like a woman's, bloated belly (כרסו צבה), protruding belly button, epilepsy (נכפה), asthma (רוח צרה), enlarged testicles (מושפן), and oversized genitalia
Rav Abba on bloated belly: story of a man who held his urine and developed a bloated belly, establishing the connection to medical harm
Tanai and Rav on urination: necessity to urinate in public even in front of many people; medical imperative overrides modesty
Story of Shmuel: needing to urinate during a public lecture; privacy provided; father's instruction to reverse the lesson to prevent harm to those without access to privacy
Rabbeinu Gershom's interpretation: father commanded Shmuel to urinate publicly to negate his previous action through a contrary action
Story of Mar bar Rav Ashi: urinating near a river despite mother-in-law approaching; his statement that medical need supersedes social consideration
Gemara's physiological understanding: two holes in males—one for urine, one for semen—separated by a thin membrane like garlic skin; holding urine can cause perforation and sterility

macro_segments.title: Theological and Medical Interpretations of Urination: Reish Lakish, Rabbi Yehoshua, and Rav Papa

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish: interpreting 'no sterile among you and your animals' as behavioral—be willing to urinate publicly like an animal to prevent biological sterility
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi: reinterpreting the verse as religious/spiritual—have students (spiritual children) and fertile prayers by humbling oneself like an animal
Rav Papa on environmental concern: do not urinate on pottery vessels or hard ground as it flows into rivers that eventually reach ארץ ישראל (Land of Israel)
Tosafot's geographical question: the direction of river flow from בבל Bavel to ארץ ישראל; requesting clarification from someone from Israel
Abaye on women: women should not urinate even in front of children; moving to the side is acceptable; less stringent than men's requirements
Tanai and Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel: holding back defecation leads to digestive illness (hadrokan); holding back urination leads to jaundice (yarkon)
Rava bar Rav Huna: comprehensive list of illnesses from bodily fluid retention—boils from excess blood, leprosy from excess semen, hadrokan from excess feces, jaundice from excess urine

topical_tags.term: אינו שוה בזרעו של אהרן (not within Aaron's seed)
male anatomy (two holes separated by thin membrane)
environmental purity (river flow to Eretz Yisrael)
